Face has many colors. So we see these beautiful things and we are struck by them. Okay. So that's the the, the one thing that, that art teaches us. The other thing that it does is, um, when we create something that is really beautiful, it just it just feeds us. I mean, we can have days when we create ugly, for sure. Okay. I have a lot of ugly things. I probably have a stack of 200 ugly paintings anyway, and I'm hoping to resurrect sometime. But. But when we create something beautiful, there is something inside of you that, um, becomes bigger than itself. I think I think creating something is quite amazing. Right? And then the other thing I learned about watercolor, that is the frustrating thing about painting in oils. That's why I still absolutely love to paint in watercolors. Okay. Is that watercolor depending on what medium you use. Okay, so if you are a gouache painter or a pastels or an oil painter, but watercolor has a mind of its own. Yes. And it makes the painting. And I talk to it and I say, okay, here's the piece of paper, and I'm going to start here. But this is up to you to decide how this is going to be, and it will do it. And it just loves it. And you just watch it flow and ebb and do its thing and, and uh, uh, and so it teaches you that you, you, you can do that with your own life. You don't have, um, stuck where you are no matter what your situation is. Watercolor is doable in any corner. You don't have to have. I now have big studios my husband has always seen. I've never had to paint at the kitchen table. Okay. but in color. I can paint the kitchen table. I can paint any place. I can paint in my car on a rainy day. Okay, watercolor can do that for you. And I learned that from the other associations that I had with with the paint watercolor, that it's something that is always there and does talk to you. Oil paint. You have to paint every brushstroke. There's no question you have you have to lay it on there. Okay. It does not create its own magic, but watercolor creates its own magic. So art is magic. If I were to say anything magic to me, and it feeds me inside in a beautiful way. Okay, so. And it makes me, um. It just makes me a better person. Okay? Because I'm working with beauty. I am so impressed with you and the stories you tell and the meaning you've drawn, and how art has influenced your family and your community. I feel really fortunate that we have this interview and thank you so much for sharing honesty. Have you got have you got one more minute and I will tell you something to do? Sure do. Of course. Okay. Okay. So I think marketing is a real trick. And I'd like to to kind of explain kind of marketing. So. So when I first started to sell watercolors, I went to Park City to a big street show. Okay. And I did a lot of street shows with my watercolors in Saint George and in, in Mesa, Arizona, and traveled all over with them. Okay. Um, but, uh, and so we had them all hung up, and it's outside. Right. So you're outside. And I told my oldest son, who was like a junior in high school, when I did this. Okay, you can have 20% of anything. Anything you sell. Okay. And so before we even opened, he came to me and he said, sold three small paintings, right. And I didn't even have my cash box open or my credit card thing. We, you know, we we managed to make it right. And anyway, so then I turned and I looked at him and he had on a old sweatshirt that had a big rip down the front, and it was held together. It was held together by a big safety pin. And I said, Tyson, what are you doing? I mean, and he said, well, I just said to him, if if my mom doesn't sell some paintings, we're not going to be able to eat. These three paintings. I was so embarrassed. I said, you get you get right back to the hotel room and change and change your outfit right now. I'm not going to have you do that. So then I had my dear mother there from Soda Springs who, you know, was was, uh, raised in, in Soda Springs, had one year of college at, uh, Idaho State University in Pocatello before she, uh, got married. And it was it was during the depression. And and so she, she lived in another with some people in their house and took care of their children. She would get up at 6:00 in the morning, start the furnace, all these things before she went to school. Uh, she played the piano for the, um, the choirs at issue to earn tuition. And half the time late to men's choir and run in there with her hair all messed up from running through the snow and all these things. It was so embarrassing. But. But inevitably, the lady that owned the house would always ask her for one more thing before she left for school. And so she had quite a interesting year at Idaho State University, and she played the piano with us. And now I can't remember his name right off. Um, but a very famous he became a very famous pianist. But anyway, she was there and, uh, and so this gentleman walked in and looked at this big painting and he said, I just really like that painting. You know, I'm thinking about buying it. Mother goes, well, I'm going to buy it. She looked up at him. She goes, I really love this painting, too. And he goes, well, I'm going to buy it right now. So he bought the painting, you know, but my mother was never going to buy this painting. I go, mother, that is so embarrassing. And later he saw us having lunch together and I go. Oh. I am cooked, But anyway, so those are some fun marketing, uh, experiences. But then I started doing, um. This took such courage. I don't I don't think anybody, um, you have to really love it to have this much courage because I'm from, you know, I'm from Soda Springs, Idaho. Right? And I go down to Taos and Santa Fe. Wow. New Mexico, with my work all by myself in my van with the whole back of it, full of my work, and have to go in and approach these galleries. And you know how gallery galleries are stuffy. Yeah, and very snooty. I want to say snooty. Okay, so you walk in. So what? I did my technique at that time. Now it would not work because now you, you submit online of course to galleries. But but at that time I had a beautiful little portfolio, um, thing that I had made that had my beautiful paintings. About ten of them in there. Okay. In, in big in like eight by tens or whatever. And I would just kind of let it hang out of the corner of my bag so you could see it, and I'd walk around and look at their work, and then the gallery gal would come up and say to me, oh, are you a painter? I say, yeah, I pull my work out. And talk to him about it. I got in a gallery in Taos doing that and and doing that and, uh, and beautiful galleries. And then I would say, well, if to see the actual work, just step outside for two minutes and look in the back of my van. And I had it all set up like a little art show in there, and they could go and see my work. And so, um, that was one of my ways of getting, uh. And then I had to approach. Then the other time, another time. This is another fun time. So I wanted to they were building the new Idaho Central Credit Union, and I really desperately wanted to get a big mural in there. And I didn't know how to do it. So I got my portfolio together and they had a meeting of about 12, really, the the president and the executives of Idaho Central Credit Union. And I had to go present my work. If you don't think I was terrified and, uh, and praying and everything else to help me. And I went in and presented my work and, uh, and I got to do their big mural that hangs in their thing, and they ended up in the long run. They have bought 32 of my big paintings for Idaho Central credit Because I had the courage to do that. Okay. And then the other time I went for the omni center that was being built, and I went in and I talked to, uh, the gentleman in charge of it, the president of, of, uh, the Omni Center. And I said, so, um, I have a nice studio up in the mountains, and I'd love to have you come up and see my work. And he said, okay, we'll come on. We set a date. Okay. And so, of course, I cleaned my house. I cleaned my studio, set all the artwork up, I had refreshments. Okay. Everything you can think of that I could think of to do. And when I opened the door, his secretary was there, and and, uh, and his wife and and he did not even come. So I showed all the way around all my artwork and everything, and I was trying to do a big mural for them also. So, um, so I was so disappointed they left and I was just really disappointed. And then he called me later in the day and he goes, all right, we have to set another date because they are so angry with me for not coming up and seeing what your work looks like. They do not want me to put cowboy art in this building. So my my work, like you saw with the landscape that won the International Landscape Award. Okay. Is on edge. Okay. And, um. And so I had to do the whole thing over again, but he did end up commissioning me for a, um, a mural. And then we took, uh, when it was time to take that down, we took ten. I painted like a maniac for, like, three weeks. And, uh, and so when we went down to install this, uh, I took ten other paintings that matched their basically their, uh, they were all landscapes but had some of their colors in it. Okay. And my husband went early and set them up at the ends of all of their blank hallways and whatever. So then we just walked, um, Mr. Hermansen around and he'd say, oh, I think we have to have that one, too. Oh, we better buy that one, too. It looks really good there. Oh, we better buy that one too. So they ended up buying all these paintings. Well, the building wasn't finished. Uh. And so we are taking these paintings and we're moving them into a storage room. And this Idaho farmer comes in and he's got his bib overalls on. Okay, now, I relate to this because my dad never wore bib overalls. My dad would have died in bib overalls, but I'm used to dealing with farmers. Right? And he was looking for the for the Farm Service Administration and thought it was in this building. And so we told him where it was and we said, you know, you better hurry because, um, uh, you know, it closes like 4:00 or whatever. And we're carrying these paintings. And he looks at them and he goes, what is this bunch of junk? Okay. My heart just went clear down to my feet because here they had just agreed to purchase these. And I thought, oh my gosh, I am dead in the water. He is not going to want these things, right? And so then he sat down on the floor over against the wall and watched us as we moved every painting in because they were big into to this storage room and then he up. I know that probably this was an answer to prayer because he stood up and he said, those are really Awesome. I really like those. You're going to put those in the building and I reprieved. So these are the kind of heart stopping things you get when you're trying to market. You have to take all of the rejection and you have to just throw it out the back door, and you just have to keep going forward. And it is not easy. But that is the hard part. Artist okay, the good part. And that's why people pay a gallery to sell their art, you know, and let the gallery take 50% because then they don't have to face these rejections. And yes, yes, but we get used to doing that. We not everyone is going to like every painting. They're not going to like them. Okay. Some people like really finished. Some people like not finished, some people, you know, like heavy paint, some people like it. We just have to do our own selves, be true to ourselves, and just do so. And I love it. So anyway, there you go. I can see my. I can see my stories that are hard. Hard one. Yeah. I think, uh, we should make this the introduction to Idaho. Every dear friend I have. Uh, well, not one of them, but so many of them have come because we share that same love of of. It's amazing. It's amazing. Yeah. So thank you. Thank you to Watercolor Society for just giving me what I needed to promote me forward. Thank you for your time, Porgy. You know this? Um. Well, this transcript will be available through the Idaho Historical Society. So if your children or grandchildren are interested in seeing it, they can tap into this any time. And, um. That's just kind of different. Okay. I'll look around. So when do you want. Will you send me when you want that to be in Boise? It probably will be like in May, something like that. Um, okay. lines.line: # Introduction and Interview Setup

> The interview begins with greetings and technical arrangements as the participants ensure they are connected and ready. Nancy Inaba introduces herself and the purpose of the interview, which is to document the history and impact of the Idaho Watercolor Society as part of a larger historical project. The formal context and goals of the oral history are established, and the speaker is thanked for her participation and background information.

metadata.title: Introduction and Interview Setup
metadata.synopsis: The interview begins with greetings and technical arrangements as the participants ensure they are connected and ready. Nancy Inaba introduces herself and the purpose of the interview, which is to document the history and impact of the Idaho Watercolor Society as part of a larger historical project. The formal context and goals of the oral history are established, and the speaker is thanked for her participation and background information.

lines.line: # Family Background and Early Life in Idaho

> The speaker shares her deep Idaho roots, describing her family's pioneering history in Soda Springs and the challenges they faced. She recounts her upbringing in a small town, her family's occupations, and her mother's musical talents. Despite the lack of formal art education in her high school, she pursued art through self-study and correspondence courses, setting the stage for her later achievements.

metadata.title: Family Background and Early Life in Idaho
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares her deep Idaho roots, describing her family's pioneering history in Soda Springs and the challenges they faced. She recounts her upbringing in a small town, her family's occupations, and her mother's musical talents. Despite the lack of formal art education in her high school, she pursued art through self-study and correspondence courses, setting the stage for her later achievements.

lines.line: # College, Marriage, and Return to Idaho

> After graduating from Utah State University, the speaker pursued a master's degree at Brigham Young University, where she met her husband. The couple lived in California for several years before returning to Idaho, motivated by their family ties and a desire to start a business. They established a rental center in Pocatello, raised five children, and built a life that balanced entrepreneurship and family.

metadata.title: College, Marriage, and Return to Idaho
metadata.synopsis: After graduating from Utah State University, the speaker pursued a master's degree at Brigham Young University, where she met her husband. The couple lived in California for several years before returning to Idaho, motivated by their family ties and a desire to start a business. They established a rental center in Pocatello, raised five children, and built a life that balanced entrepreneurship and family.

lines.line: # Early Involvement with Idaho Watercolor Society

> Upon returning to Idaho, the speaker quickly became involved with the Idaho Watercolor Society. She describes the excitement of participating in juried shows with nationally recognized artists and the value of workshops that expanded her artistic horizons. The society provided her with vital connections, friendships, and exposure to the broader art world, which were instrumental in her development as an artist.

metadata.title: Early Involvement with Idaho Watercolor Society
metadata.synopsis: Upon returning to Idaho, the speaker quickly became involved with the Idaho Watercolor Society. She describes the excitement of participating in juried shows with nationally recognized artists and the value of workshops that expanded her artistic horizons. The society provided her with vital connections, friendships, and exposure to the broader art world, which were instrumental in her development as an artist.

lines.line: # Family Life, Art, and Watercolor Society Events

> The speaker highlights the support of her husband, who built her multiple studios, and shares anecdotes about attending society events with her children. She recalls the camaraderie and creative energy of annual meetings, workshops, and musical gatherings, emphasizing how these experiences enriched both her family life and her artistic journey. The society became a source of friendship, inspiration, and professional growth.

metadata.title: Family Life, Art, and Watercolor Society Events
metadata.synopsis: The speaker highlights the support of her husband, who built her multiple studios, and shares anecdotes about attending society events with her children. She recalls the camaraderie and creative energy of annual meetings, workshops, and musical gatherings, emphasizing how these experiences enriched both her family life and her artistic journey. The society became a source of friendship, inspiration, and professional growth.

lines.line: # Achievements, Awards, and Artistic Growth

> The speaker details her artistic accomplishments, including winning prestigious awards and being featured in national and international exhibitions. She credits the Idaho Watercolor Society for providing the skills, techniques, and encouragement that propelled her to success. The society's connection to larger organizations, such as the Western Federation of Watercolor Societies, further broadened her opportunities and recognition.

metadata.title: Achievements, Awards, and Artistic Growth
metadata.synopsis: The speaker details her artistic accomplishments, including winning prestigious awards and being featured in national and international exhibitions. She credits the Idaho Watercolor Society for providing the skills, techniques, and encouragement that propelled her to success. The society's connection to larger organizations, such as the Western Federation of Watercolor Societies, further broadened her opportunities and recognition.

lines.line: # Teaching, Community, and Balancing Art with Family

> Teaching workshops became a key motivator for the speaker, pushing her to continually develop her skills and share knowledge with others. She describes strategies for balancing her artistic pursuits with raising five children, such as painting early in the morning and prioritizing studio time over housework. The support of her family and the local art community, including the Pocatello Art Center, played a crucial role in sustaining her creative practice.

metadata.title: Teaching, Community, and Balancing Art with Family
metadata.synopsis: Teaching workshops became a key motivator for the speaker, pushing her to continually develop her skills and share knowledge with others. She describes strategies for balancing her artistic pursuits with raising five children, such as painting early in the morning and prioritizing studio time over housework. The support of her family and the local art community, including the Pocatello Art Center, played a crucial role in sustaining her creative practice.

lines.line: # Entering Shows, Marketing, and Transition to Oils

> The speaker discusses the challenges of marketing art and the practical decision to shift from watercolor to oil painting, which proved more lucrative and marketable. She shares insights on tailoring submissions to jurors' preferences and the importance of self-promotion, noting that artistic talent alone is not enough for success. Her experiences with entering shows and adapting to the art market reflect the realities faced by many artists.

metadata.title: Entering Shows, Marketing, and Transition to Oils
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the challenges of marketing art and the practical decision to shift from watercolor to oil painting, which proved more lucrative and marketable. She shares insights on tailoring submissions to jurors' preferences and the importance of self-promotion, noting that artistic talent alone is not enough for success. Her experiences with entering shows and adapting to the art market reflect the realities faced by many artists.

lines.line: # Major Exhibitions and Family Involvement in Art

> The speaker recounts her solo exhibition at the Museum of Eastern Idaho, where she showcased a diverse range of works across multiple galleries. She describes involving her grandchildren in art sales, teaching them entrepreneurial skills, and using her art to benefit the community, such as raising funds for the Pocatello Art Center. These experiences illustrate the intergenerational impact of her art and her commitment to community engagement.

metadata.title: Major Exhibitions and Family Involvement in Art
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts her solo exhibition at the Museum of Eastern Idaho, where she showcased a diverse range of works across multiple galleries. She describes involving her grandchildren in art sales, teaching them entrepreneurial skills, and using her art to benefit the community, such as raising funds for the Pocatello Art Center. These experiences illustrate the intergenerational impact of her art and her commitment to community engagement.

lines.line: # Regional Unity and Organizational Structure

> The speaker addresses the challenges and strengths of maintaining unity within the Idaho Watercolor Society across different regions. She emphasizes the value of annual meetings, rotating state shows, and collaborative efforts among regional representatives. The society's structure fosters connections, knowledge sharing, and a sense of belonging among members from various parts of Idaho.

metadata.title: Regional Unity and Organizational Structure
metadata.synopsis: The speaker addresses the challenges and strengths of maintaining unity within the Idaho Watercolor Society across different regions. She emphasizes the value of annual meetings, rotating state shows, and collaborative efforts among regional representatives. The society's structure fosters connections, knowledge sharing, and a sense of belonging among members from various parts of Idaho.

lines.line: # The Value of Painting Together and Artistic Community

> Regular group painting sessions and informal gatherings are highlighted as essential for artistic growth and community building. The speaker shares creative strategies for plein air outings and the benefits of learning from peers. These shared experiences foster camaraderie, mutual support, and continuous learning, reinforcing the importance of community in sustaining artistic practice.

metadata.title: The Value of Painting Together and Artistic Community
metadata.synopsis: Regular group painting sessions and informal gatherings are highlighted as essential for artistic growth and community building. The speaker shares creative strategies for plein air outings and the benefits of learning from peers. These shared experiences foster camaraderie, mutual support, and continuous learning, reinforcing the importance of community in sustaining artistic practice.

lines.line: # International Experiences and Artistic Influence

> The speaker describes her time in Mexico, where she painted portraits for fellow missionaries, and her transformative experience studying art in Russia. These international experiences broadened her artistic perspective, introduced her to new techniques, and influenced her teaching style. She reflects on the differences between French and Russian Impressionism and how travel and exposure to other cultures have enriched her art.

metadata.title: International Experiences and Artistic Influence
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes her time in Mexico, where she painted portraits for fellow missionaries, and her transformative experience studying art in Russia. These international experiences broadened her artistic perspective, introduced her to new techniques, and influenced her teaching style. She reflects on the differences between French and Russian Impressionism and how travel and exposure to other cultures have enriched her art.

lines.line: # Overcoming Limitations and Rejection in Art

> The speaker reflects on the challenges of facing rejection and the emotional ups and downs of entering art shows. She shares personal anecdotes about overcoming setbacks, including humorous stories about double entries and the anxiety of awaiting results. Her perseverance and willingness to learn from each experience underscore the resilience required to succeed as an artist.

metadata.title: Overcoming Limitations and Rejection in Art
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the challenges of facing rejection and the emotional ups and downs of entering art shows. She shares personal anecdotes about overcoming setbacks, including humorous stories about double entries and the anxiety of awaiting results. Her perseverance and willingness to learn from each experience underscore the resilience required to succeed as an artist.

lines.line: # The Meaning and Impact of Art

> The speaker offers deep reflections on the transformative power of art, emphasizing how it enhances perception, fosters creativity, and brings personal fulfillment. She discusses the unique qualities of watercolor, its unpredictability, and the sense of magic it brings to her life. Art, for her, is a source of beauty, growth, and connection to others, making her a better person and enriching her daily existence.

metadata.title: The Meaning and Impact of Art

She discusses the unique qualities of watercolor, its unpredictability, and the sense of magic it brings to her life. Art, for her, is a source of beauty, growth, and connection to others, making her a better person and enriching her daily existence.

lines.line: # Marketing Experiences and Courage in Art Business

> The speaker recounts her varied experiences with marketing her art, from street shows and family sales tactics to approaching galleries and securing large commissions. She highlights the courage required to promote oneself, navigate rejection, and persist in the business side of art. Her stories illustrate the practical challenges artists face and the importance of resilience, adaptability, and self-belief in achieving success.

lines.line: # Closing Remarks and Future Events

> The interview concludes with expressions of gratitude, logistical details about the transcript and recording, and plans for upcoming Idaho Watercolor Society events. The speaker is invited to participate in the 50th anniversary celebration, with opportunities to reconnect with old friends and showcase her work. The conversation ends on a note of appreciation and anticipation for future gatherings.

> The speaker reflects on the challenges of facing rejection and the emotional ups and downs of entering art shows. She shares personal anecdotes about overcoming setbacks, including humorous stories about double entries and the anxiety of awaiting results. Her perseverance and willingness to learn from each experience underscore the resilience required to succeed as an artist.
# The Meaning and Impact of Art

> The speaker offers deep reflections on the transformative power of art, emphasizing how it enhances perception, fosters creativity, and brings personal fulfillment. She discusses the unique qualities of watercolor, its unpredictability, and the sense of magic it brings to her life. Art, for her, is a source of beauty, growth, and connection to others, making her a better person and enriching her daily existence.
# Marketing Experiences and Courage in Art Business

> The speaker recounts her varied experiences with marketing her art, from street shows and family sales tactics to approaching galleries and securing large commissions. She highlights the courage required to promote oneself, navigate rejection, and persist in the business side of art. Her stories illustrate the practical challenges artists face and the importance of resilience, adaptability, and self-belief in achieving success.
# Closing Remarks and Future Events

> The interview concludes with expressions of gratitude, logistical details about the transcript and recording, and plans for upcoming Idaho Watercolor Society events. The speaker is invited to participate in the 50th anniversary celebration, with opportunities to reconnect with old friends and showcase her work. The conversation ends on a note of appreciation and anticipation for future gatherings.

story.title: Kathleen Torgensen Murdock
story.description: Nancy Inaba and Kathleen "Torgensen" Murdock discuss her experiences as an artist and as a member of the Idaho Water Color Society.
